An early psychophysical invariant is Weber's ratio which can be subsumed by the expression 
                         
                            (1) 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        Δ 
                                        ( 
                                        a

                               c

                         1 
                      
                      
                         1

                       (measured in ratio scale units, e.g., length in cm, weight in g, or tone intensity in  
                         watts 
                         / 
                         
                            
                               m 
                            
                            
                               2

                       is that difference in intensity which is  just noticeably different  for an individual, and  c  is a constant that depends, for example, on the observer and the sensory modality.  Fechner, in  Elemente der Psychophysik  (1860/1889) , made an attempt to establish  Eq. (1)  as a general law, although, in some cases (e.g., perception of weights lifted by hand), Fechner observed small but systematic deviations from Weber's law. At the beginning of the twentieth century, it became clear that experimental data rarely obey  Eq. (1) .  Guilford (1932)  showed that the power law 
                         
                            (2) 
                            
                               Δ

                               a

                       less than 1, fits empirical data much better than Weber's law.

In order to fit  Eqs. (1) and (2)  to empirical data,  
                         Δ 
                         ( 
                         a 
                         ) 
                       has to be defined statistically, that is, a unique discrimination probability has to be fixed by the experimenter. However, since there are theoretical problems involved in specifying such a criterion, today, Weber's law is most often expressed in terms of a sensitivity function  
                         ξ

In practice, the stimulus intensity

                               π 
                            
                         
                         (

                       can be estimated by means of standard experimental procedures like, for example, adaptive staircase procedures ( Kaernbach, 1991; Levitt, 1971 , for a comprehensive overview see  Leek, 2001 ). Since, in order to obtain an estimate of  
                         
                            
                               ξ 
                            
                            
                               π 
                            
                         
                         ( 
                         a 
                         ) 
                      , the stimulus intensity  a  must be held constant throughout a block of trials,  a  is usually referred to as  standard stimulus  or  referent . In each trial of an adaptive staircase procedure, a subject has to compare the intensity of the standard stimulus with the intensity of a  comparison stimulus . Then, the intensity of the comparison stimulus is changed according to the responses of the subject and, finally, an estimate of  
                         
                            
                               ξ 
                            
                            
                               π 
                            
                         
                         ( 
                         a 
                         ) 
                       is computed, most commonly by averaging the intensities of the comparison stimuli at the observed turn points ( Leek, 2001 ).

It is important to note here that the standard stimulus  a  and the comparison stimulus  x  belong to different  observation areas  ( Dzhafarov, 2002, 2003 ). For instance, in a paired-comparison experiment, the standard stimulus  a  may be presented first, followed by the comparison stimulus  x  (or vice versa). Alternatively, if we are dealing with visual objects, then  a  may be presented to the left and  x  to the right of a fixation mark ( Dzhafarov, 2002 ). Obviously, the standard stimulus may or may not be assigned to different observation areas. To emphasize the fact that this assignment can have an effect on the outcome of an experiment,  Davidson and Beaver's (1977)  notation is adopted for the moment: Let

                         3

Recent data by  Augustin and Roscher (2007)  indicate that the near-miss-to-Weber's law holds in the field of visual perception as well: In a two-alternative forced-choice experiment, participants were required to compare the perceived area of squares presented on a computer screen. All results are in good correspondence with pure tone intensity discrimination data: (a) the near-miss-to-Weber's law fits the empirical data of all 14 participants, (b) the exponent  
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         ) 
                       is a non-constant function of the criterion value  
                         π 
                      , and (c) the parameters

                         ( 
                         π 
                         ) 
                       and

                         ( 
                         π 
                         ) 
                       co-vary according to  Eq. (7) . This provides empirical evidence that the near-miss-to-Weber's law is a general law that holds across different sensory modalities.

In the present article, we also approach the near-miss-to-Weber's law  (6)  in the context of psychometric models of discrimination. However, different from  Aczél and Falmagne (1999)  and  Iverson (2006) , the models are not based on Fechnerian psychophysics, but are rather motivated by the fact that psychometric functions are usually assumed to be sigmoid functions such as the distribution function of the Weibull, logistic, normal, or Gumbel distribution ( Strasburger, 2001; Treutwein, 1995; Treutwein & Strasburger, 1999; Wichmann & Hill, 2001 ). To formalize this idea, we introduce two different types of sensitivity functions, characterized by the representations  
                         
                            
                               P 
                            
                            
                               a 
                            
                         
                         ( 
                         x 
                         ) 
                         = 
                         F 
                         ( 
                         ρ

                         a 
                         ) 
                         
                            
                               x

                               ( 
                               a 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                         ) 
                       (type  A ), and  
                         
                            
                               P 
                            
                            
                               a 
                            
                         
                         ( 
                         x 
                         ) 
                         = 
                         F 
                         ( 
                         γ 
                         ( 
                         a 
                         ) 
                         + 
                         ρ 
                         ( 
                         a 
                         ) 
                         x 
                         ) 
                       (type  B ). It is shown that both representations are compatible with the near-miss-to-Weber's law ( Theorems 4  and  6 ). Furthermore, if a sensitivity function of type  A  satisfies the near-miss-to-Weber's law, then the exponent  
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         ) 
                       is either a real valued constant, or the near-miss can be specialized into the fixed-point-model ( Theorem 5 ). If, on the other hand, the near-miss-to-Weber's law holds for a sensitivity function of type  B , then  
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         ) 
                       is necessarily a constant function of the criterion value  
                         π 
                       ( Theorem 7 ).

The article is organized as follows: In  Section 2 , we introduce basic definitions and elementary preliminaries which are background to the rest of this paper.  Section 3  contains the main results, and the mathematical proofs are presented in the final  Section 4 .

For  
                         0 
                         < 
                         p 
                         < 
                         q 
                         < 
                         1 
                      , let  
                         P 
                         ≔ 
                         [ 
                         p 
                         , 
                         q 
                         ] 
                       be a set of discrimination probabilities (subsequently referred to as criterion values). Similarly, for  
                         τ 
                         > 
                         1 
                      , let  
                         I 
                         = 
                         [ 
                         1 
                         , 
                         τ 
                         ] 
                       be a set of stimulus intensities. The elements in  I  correspond to the physical intensities of the standard stimuli presented in a paired comparison experiment. Since the lowest intensity is denoted by 1, the smallest standard stimulus is used as measurement unit. Furthermore, for any combination of standard stimulus  
                         a 
                         ∈ 
                         I 
                       and criterion value  
                         π 
                         ∈ 
                         P 
                      , there exists a unique stimulus intensity  
                         
                            
                               ξ 
                            
                            
                               π 
                            
                         
                         ( 
                         a 
                         ) 
                         > 
                         0 
                       which is judged greater than the standard stimulus  a  with probability  
                         π 
                      . In practice, the stimulus intensity  
                         
                            
                               ξ 
                            
                            
                               π 
                            
                         
                         ( 
                         a 
                         ) 
                       can be estimated by means of standard experimental procedures like, for example, adaptive staircase procedures (cf. Section 1).

The aim of the present section is to examine the near-miss-to-Weber's law in the context of psychometric models of discrimination. On the one hand, we show that the near-miss is compatible with both sensitivity functions of  Definition 3 . On the other hand, we analyze whether the exponent  
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         ) 
                       in the near-miss-to-Weber's law is a constant function of the criterion value  
                         π 
                      . Finally, if  
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         ) 
                       is a non-constant function of  
                         π 
                      , then the relationship between the parameter functions  
                         π 
                         ↦ 
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         ) 
                       and  
                         π 
                         ↦ 
                         K 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         ) 
                       is explored. To begin with,  Theorem 4  shows that the near-miss is compatible with the definition of a sensitivity function of type  A :

Finally, it has to be noted that  Theorem 5  is based on two critical assumptions on the shape of the parameter function  
                         π

                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         )

                         π

                         π

                         π 
                      , which is in accordance with the empirical findings by  Doble et al. (2006)  and  Augustin and Roscher (2007) .

Theorem 7  has far reaching consequences for the modelling of intensity discrimination data: If the near-miss-to-Weber's law holds with a non-constant parameter function  
                         π 
                         ↦ 
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         ) 
                      , then the psychometric functions cannot be represented as  
                         
                            
                               P 
                            
                            
                               a 
                            
                         
                         ( 
                         x 
                         ) 
                         = 
                         F 
                         ( 
                         γ 
                         ( 
                         a 
                         ) 
                         + 
                         ρ 
                         ( 
                         a 
                         ) 
                         x 
                         ) 
                      . If, on the contrary, the psychometric functions are represented as  
                         
                            
                               P 
                            
                            
                               a 
                            
                         
                         ( 
                         x 
                         ) 
                         = 
                         F 
                         ( 
                         γ 
                         ( 
                         a 
                         ) 
                         + 
                         ρ 
                         ( 
                         a 
                         ) 
                         x 
                         ) 
                       (as it is the case for the distribution functions of the logistic, Gaussian, and Gumbel distribution), then the near-miss can only hold with a constant exponent  
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         ) 
                      . Since there is empirical evidence that the exponent in the near-miss-to-Weber's law varies with the criterion value ( Augustin & Roscher, 2007; Doble et al., 2006 ),  Theorem 4  supports a representation of the form 
                         
                            (20) 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     P 
                                  
                                  
                                     a 
                                  
                               
                               ( 
                               x

                               F

                               a 
                               )

                                     γ 
                                     ( 
                                     a 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                               , 
                               
                               a 
                               ∈ 
                               I 
                               , 
                               
                               x 
                               ∈ 
                               
                                  
                                     I 
                                  
                                  
                                     a 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                            
                         
                      which is consistent with a non-constant parameter function  
                         π 
                         ↦ 
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         ) 
                      . Consequently,  Theorem 5  provides evidence that the near-miss-to-Weber's law can be specialized into the fixed-point-model  
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     ξ 
                                  
                                  
                                     π

                                              π

                                     a

                                     ( 
                                     π 
                                     )

                               ∀ 
                               a 
                               ∈ 
                               I

                               ∀ 
                               π

                               P

                      with a non-constant exponent  
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         ) 
                      . This is in accordance with the theoretical results by  Aczél and Falmagne (1999)  and  Iverson (2006) , and the empirical findings by  Doble et al. (2006)  and  Augustin and Roscher (2007) . However, in order to reach a final conclusion, further empirical work is necessary.
